APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper sits squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues: it addresses reading comprehension / cloze-style QA using novel model architecture (Gated-Attention multi-hop reader) and evaluates on standard datasets (CNN, Daily Mail, CBT, WDW) against strong baselines (AS Reader, BiDAF, MemNets, ReasoNet, etc.). The methodology and evaluation metrics (accuracy, cross-entropy training objective) match community standards, and the thorough related work coverage indicates correct positioning for the venue. While some experimental reporting details (e.g., hyperparameters, code availability) are not mentioned in the summary, that does not undermine the paper’s topical fit for a top conference. | B(5): This paper is an excellent fit for a top-tier NLP/ML venue: it tackles reading comprehension with a novel gated-attention multi-hop architecture and demonstrates state-of-the-art results on standard benchmarks (CNN, Daily Mail, CBT-NE/CN, WDW). The work uses community-standard methods and evaluations, compares against strong baselines (AS Reader, BiDAF, MemNets, ReasoNet, etc.), and includes ablations showing the effectiveness of the multiplicative gating — all qualities I prize as an empiricist. While code and hyperparameter details would further strengthen reproducibility, the empirical contribution and positioning are squarely appropriate for the venue.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper sits squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues: it addresses reading comprehension / cloze-style QA using novel model architecture (Gated-Attention multi-hop reader) and evaluates on standard datasets (CNN, Daily Mail, CBT, WDW) against strong baselines (AS Reader, BiDAF, MemNets, ReasoNet, etc.). The methodology and evaluation metrics (accuracy, cross-entropy training objective) match community standards, and the thorough related work coverage indicates correct positioning for the venue. While some experimental reporting details (e.g., hyperparameters, code availability) are not mentioned in the summary, that does not undermine the paper’s topical fit for a top conference.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is an excellent fit for a top-tier NLP/ML venue: it tackles reading comprehension with a novel gated-attention multi-hop architecture and demonstrates state-of-the-art results on standard benchmarks (CNN, Daily Mail, CBT-NE/CN, WDW). The work uses community-standard methods and evaluations, compares against strong baselines (AS Reader, BiDAF, MemNets, ReasoNet, etc.), and includes ablations showing the effectiveness of the multiplicative gating — all qualities I prize as an empiricist. While code and hyperparameter details would further strengthen reproducibility, the empirical contribution and positioning are squarely appropriate for the venue.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against a broad and recent set of baselines (AS Reader, AoA, MemNets, NSE, ReasoNet, EpiReader, BiDAF, etc.) on the same standard cloze datasets (CNN, Daily Mail, CBT, WDW) using the same accuracy metric, and it includes ablations and operator comparisons. However, the summary does not report hyperparameter/experimental details, multiple seeds, or statistical significance testing, which I (as a theory-focused reviewer) view as important for fair claims of superiority. Overall the empirical comparison is strong and comprehensive in breadth but lacks the rigorous reporting needed to fully assess fairness and robustness. | B(4): The paper compares against a broad and recent set of strong baselines (AS Reader, AoA, BiDAF, MemNets, EpiReader, ReasoNet, DER, NSE, etc.) across standard RC benchmarks (CNN, Daily Mail, CBT-NE/CN, WDW) using accuracy, which is appropriate. It also includes targeted ablations (e.g., multiplicative interactions vs. alternatives) and appears to analyze differences in model design. Minor shortcomings: code/hyperparameter details aren’t reported in the summary (which complicates exact reproducibility and fairness checks), and the summary does not explicitly state whether the paper acknowledges cases where prior methods match or exceed performance. Overall the empirical comparison is strong with only modest omissions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a broad and recent set of baselines (AS Reader, AoA, MemNets, NSE, ReasoNet, EpiReader, BiDAF, etc.) on the same standard cloze datasets (CNN, Daily Mail, CBT, WDW) using the same accuracy metric, and it includes ablations and operator comparisons. However, the summary does not report hyperparameter/experimental details, multiple seeds, or statistical significance testing, which I (as a theory-focused reviewer) view as important for fair claims of superiority. Overall the empirical comparison is strong and comprehensive in breadth but lacks the rigorous reporting needed to fully assess fairness and robustness.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a broad and recent set of strong baselines (AS Reader, AoA, BiDAF, MemNets, EpiReader, ReasoNet, DER, NSE, etc.) across standard RC benchmarks (CNN, Daily Mail, CBT-NE/CN, WDW) using accuracy, which is appropriate. It also includes targeted ablations (e.g., multiplicative interactions vs. alternatives) and appears to analyze differences in model design. Minor shortcomings: code/hyperparameter details aren’t reported in the summary (which complicates exact reproducibility and fairness checks), and the summary does not explicitly state whether the paper acknowledges cases where prior methods match or exceed performance. Overall the empirical comparison is strong with only modest omissions.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and reads clearly: the multi-hop GA Reader architecture, gated-attention mechanism, and experimental comparisons are presented in a logical intro→method→experiments→conclusion flow, and the main contributions are stated early. Notation and methodology are consistently described (Bi-GRU encoders, layer-wise multiplicative gating, pointer-sum aggregation), and related work coverage and figures/tables/examples appear effective and informative. Minor presentation gaps remain: hyperparameter details and experimental variance across seeds are not reported, and no limitations are explicitly discussed—these omissions reduce reproducibility and should be called out, but do not severely impair clarity. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the flow from introduction to method to experiments is clear, with the GA Reader’s main contributions stated early and consistently tied to the multi-hop gated-attention design. Notation appears consistent and related work coverage is thorough; figures, tables and examples are described as effective and the writing quality is good. Minor presentation shortcomings are the lack of reported hyperparameter details and absence of stated limitations or code availability, which would help reproducibility and make some experimental claims easier to verify.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and reads clearly: the multi-hop GA Reader architecture, gated-attention mechanism, and experimental comparisons are presented in a logical intro→method→experiments→conclusion flow, and the main contributions are stated early. Notation and methodology are consistently described (Bi-GRU encoders, layer-wise multiplicative gating, pointer-sum aggregation), and related work coverage and figures/tables/examples appear effective and informative. Minor presentation gaps remain: hyperparameter details and experimental variance across seeds are not reported, and no limitations are explicitly discussed—these omissions reduce reproducibility and should be called out, but do not severely impair clarity.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the flow from introduction to method to experiments is clear, with the GA Reader’s main contributions stated early and consistently tied to the multi-hop gated-attention design. Notation appears consistent and related work coverage is thorough; figures, tables and examples are described as effective and the writing quality is good. Minor presentation shortcomings are the lack of reported hyperparameter details and absence of stated limitations or code availability, which would help reproducibility and make some experimental claims easier to verify.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper introduces a clear architectural novelty—layer-wise multiplicative gated-attention producing query-specific token representations—while operating within a well-studied problem and lacking theoretical analysis. Given the non-trivial empirical gains, focused ablations, and practical interest to the community, the contribution is more than incremental engineering but not a paradigm shift; therefore a score of 4 best reflects its notable methodological novelty and practical impact.

reviewer_a.justification: The work combines existing ideas—multi-hop representation learning and attention-based reading—with a layer-wise multiplicative gated-attention mechanism to produce query-specific token representations; this is a concrete architectural twist rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. While the element-wise multiplicative gating and its application per token across hops is a non-trivial engineering contribution that is clearly differentiated from prior readers and supported empirically on multiple benchmarks (CNN, Daily Mail, CBT, WDW), there is no formal/theoretical analysis to explain why multiplicative gating should be preferred, nor does the formulation introduce a new problem. From a theory-oriented perspective the contribution reads as an incremental but useful extension rather than a highly original idea that would surprise an expert.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a distinct architectural mechanism—layer-wise Gated-Attention that forms query-specific token representations via element-wise multiplicative interactions within a multi-hop reader—which is a clear methodological novelty relative to prior cloze readers (AS Reader, AoA, BiDAF, etc.). The authors differentiate their contribution with focused ablations comparing compositional operators and demonstrate SOTA results on multiple benchmarks, indicating a non-trivial and practically useful extension rather than a mere combination of existing pieces. The problem setting is well-studied (cloze-style RC), so the novelty lies in the model design and empirical validation, which would be of interest to practitioners and experts.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important problem and achieves strong state-of-the-art empirical results across multiple benchmarks, making it likely to influence follow-on applied QA work. However, the lack of theoretical grounding and incomplete reproducibility details limits its transformational impact, so a slightly above-moderate score reflects its practical significance tempered by these shortcomings.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important problem—cloze-style reading comprehension—and demonstrates strong empirical gains (state-of-the-art on CNN, Daily Mail, CBT, WDW) using a multi-hop reader with layerwise multiplicative gated-attention. However, from the summary there is no theoretical analysis or provable properties explaining why element-wise multiplicative gating yields these improvements, and key reproducibility details (hyperparameters/code) are missing. As a result, while the empirical results make the approach useful and likely to be tried by others, its impact is moderate rather than transformative because it lacks principled grounding and broader explanatory insight.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important practical problem—cloze-style reading comprehension—by proposing a well-motivated GA Reader that combines multi-hop modeling with element-wise multiplicative gated attention. Empirically it achieves state-of-the-art results across multiple strong benchmarks (CNN, Daily Mail, WDW) and includes ablations showing the effectiveness of the multiplicative interactions, which makes the contribution convincing and likely to influence follow-on architectures. While not a theoretical breakthrough, the method is an engineering-forward advance that is reproducible in spirit and likely to be widely adopted or extended in applied QA work, though lack of reported code/hyperparameters slightly limits immediate uptake.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper’s claims are clear and empirically supported, the model formulation is correct, and ablations isolate the key effect, but they also note missing reproducibility details (hyperparameters, code, random-seed variance) and lack of theoretical analysis or error analysis. Given the solid empirical evidence and correct technical presentation but the noted gaps that reduce confidence in generality and reproducibility, a score of 4 appropriately reflects strong soundness with some important methodological limitations.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents clear claims and experimental evidence (state-of-the-art results on CNN/DailyMail/CBT/WDW) and includes an ablation demonstrating the benefit of multiplicative gated-attention; however, there is no formal theoretical analysis or proofs of the proposed mechanism’s properties. The experimental methodology lacks reported hyperparameter settings, variance across random seeds, or statistical significance testing, which weakens confidence in claimed gains. While the architecture and reported comparisons to strong baselines appear technically sound, the absence of thorough error analysis, reproducibility details, and theoretical grounding leaves some concerns about the robustness and generality of the conclusions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and empirically supported: a multi-hop GA Reader with multiplicative gating achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on multiple cloze-style benchmarks and includes ablations that isolate the gating effect. There are no complex mathematical proofs required and the model formulation is straightforward and correct as presented. Experimental methodology is strong in terms of baselines and datasets, but the absence of reported hyperparameters/code and no discussion of limitations weakens reproducibility and makes it harder to fully validate claims. Overall the technical contribution is solid with only minor issues around experimental transparency.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper delivers strong empirical results across multiple benchmarks with useful ablations showing the value of multiplicative gated interactions. However, they also both note important omissions—lack of hyperparameter/seed reporting, no significance testing or released code, and absence of failure-mode discussion—which limit reproducibility and interpretability. Given the solid empirical contribution but these methodological gaps, a score of 4 reflects the paper’s strengths while penalizing the missing reproducibility and analysis details.

reviewer_a.justification: The experimental evaluation is adequate — multiple strong benchmarks (CNN, Daily Mail, CBT-NE/CN, WDW) and competitive baselines are compared, and an ablation study highlights the importance of multiplicative (gated) interactions. However, critical methodological details are missing for a principled assessment: hyperparameters and reproducibility information are not reported, there are no significance tests or variance across random seeds, and failure modes/limitations are not discussed. From a theory-oriented perspective the paper also lacks formal analysis explaining why the gated-attention should provably or mechanistically improve multi-hop inference, leaving the work empirically driven without deeper explanation.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents strong empirical evidence with state-of-the-art results on multiple benchmarks (CNN, Daily Mail, CBT-NE/CN, WDW) and compares against a wide set of competitive baselines, so experiments are largely comprehensive. It includes targeted ablations demonstrating the value of multiplicative (gated) interactions, and related work coverage is thorough. However, the paper omits discussion of failure cases or limitations and does not report hyperparameters or release code, which weakens reproducibility. Given solid empirical claims and ablations but missing reproducibility details and negative-result discussion, I rate it a 4.

main_claims: Proposes the Gated-Attention (GA) Reader that integrates a multi-hop architecture with a gated-attention mechanism based on multiplicative interactions between query and document token representations.
The GA Reader builds query-specific token representations via element-wise (multiplicative) gating applied layer-wise during multi-hop representation learning.
Demonstrates state-of-the-art results on three benchmarks for cloze-style QA: CNN, Daily Mail, and Who Did What.
Shows the effectiveness of multiplicative interactions via an ablation study and comparisons to alternative compositional operators.
_summary.methodology: A multi-hop reader using Bi-GRU encoders for document and query, with a layer-wise Gated-Attention module that forms a soft-attended query representation per document token and multiplies it element-wise with the token representation; final token scores are produced by dot-product with the query representation at the cloze position and aggregated with pointer-sum attention over candidate occurrences.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - reading comprehension / cloze-style question answering

reviewer_a.name: Theory Purist
reviewer_a.background: Associate professor in ML/NLP with strong background in model theory and representation learning; long experience reviewing for ACL/ICLR focusing on principled contributions.
traits: Penalises papers that provide empirical gains without formal or theoretical justification (will deduct points if there is no theoretical analysis or provable property).
Requires thorough statistical testing and confidence intervals for reported improvements (will penalise if no significance tests or variance across seeds).
Demands detailed error analysis and interpretability for novel architectural components (will deduct points if only quantitative numbers and superficial ablation are provided).
Penalises incremental architectural tweaks that lack clear conceptual novelty (will downgrade if contribution appears to be an engineering variant rather than a new principle).
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on conceptual novelty and theoretical grounding; gives low scores to empirically-driven papers that lack analysis, and suggests experiments that reveal underlying mechanisms.

reviewer_b.name: Empirical Systems Enthusiast
reviewer_b.background: Senior research scientist building applied NLP models for question answering and information extraction; frequent reviewer for EMNLP/ACL valuing practical advances and strong empirical evidence.
traits: Rewards papers that achieve state-of-the-art results on standard benchmarks (will give bonus for clear SOTA improvements on multiple datasets).
Values thorough empirical ablations that isolate the contribution of components (will upscore if ablation demonstrates the claimed mechanism works).
Prefers reproducible, well-engineered models and clear training/implementation details (will upscore when code, hyperparameters, and sensible baselines are provided).
Less stringent about formal proofs or deep theory; tolerant of engineering-oriented innovations that deliver practical gains (will not penalise lack of theory if empirical case is strong).
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Pragmatic evaluator who rewards solid empirical demonstration, reproducibility and clear ablations; willing to accept engineering-centric contributions if they lead to meaningful improvements.
